text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Kate
Health Centre

Keele University, Keele, Newcastle

 Staffordshire ST5 5BG
Dear Dr Kate
Wendy Brown, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: K482715 NHS No. 6592841037
Flat 7 Brookside Avenue, Newcastle-under-Lyme, ST5 2QD 

Diagnoses/summary: Recurrent unprovoked seizures; photosensitivity to flicker/bright light noted clinically and by patient report; prior EEGs mixed/indeterminate; MRI previously non-diagnostic.

Neuroimaging: MRI brain (2018) reported no structural lesion; no clear epileptogenic focus identified.

EEG: Routine EEGs in 2019 and 2021 inconclusive; photic stimulation elicited discomfort and brief eyelid flutter without definitive epileptiform discharges.

Medication history:
- Current: Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily; Clobazam 10 mg nocte; Folic Acid 5 mg daily; Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily; PRN Prochlorperazine for nausea.
- Previous: Levetiracetam (mood change/irritability), Carbamazepine (dizziness, hyponatraemia), Sodium Valproate (stopped due to family planning discussion), Lacosamide (limited benefit), Gabapentin (sedation), Topiramate (cognitive slowing, paraesthesia), Zonisamide (loss of appetite, low mood), Perampanel (aggression), Tiagabine (tremor), Vigabatrin (not trialled), Rufinamide (not trialled), Brivaracetam (fatigue), Pregabalin (weight gain).

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

I reviewed Wendy Brown in clinic today. They attended with their partner. Since the spring, they describe clustering of episodes predominantly towards bedtime—most evenings as they settle down for the night, with additional events on some early mornings when sleep is fragmented. They phrase it as “nearly every night before sleep and now and then at first light,” which aligns with the diary they brought. There are also occasional brief events triggered by bright, flickering light in shops and on screens; they have begun using tinted lenses and screen filters with partial benefit. They deny prolonged convulsions; typical events last under two minutes, with brief confusion afterwards. No injuries since the last visit.

Intercurrent issues: Ongoing fatigue, intermittent headaches, and increased stress at work. They have reduced caffeine. Sleep is variable; partner reports snoring but no witnessed apnoeas. No recent infections. Menstrual cycles reported regular; considering pregnancy in the next 1–2 years and has requested pre-conception counselling.

Examination today was unremarkable. Weight stable. No focal neurological signs.

Risk/safety: No driving. Rescue plan reviewed. Photosensitivity precautions reinforced (avoid strobe/flicker; gradual screen brightness, larger fonts; consider blue-blocking filters). Household has smoke/CO alarms and a shower chair. 

Discussion and plan:
- We discussed the pattern of episodes clustering around bedtime/first light and with bright light exposure. Given tolerability of lamotrigine and partial response, we will cautiously optimise dosing: increase to 125 mg twice daily for two weeks, then 150 mg twice daily if well tolerated. Adverse effects discussed (rash including Stevens–Johnson syndrome, dizziness, diplopia, mood change). Patient has had no prior rash on lamotrigine; safety-netting advice given.
- Continue Clobazam 10 mg nocte; may use an extra 5–10 mg on particularly high-risk evenings (e.g. anticipated flicker exposure or poor sleep) no more than 2–3 times per week; monitor sedation.
- Photosensitivity measures: prescribed tinted lenses (brown/grey) and provided advice on display refresh rates, anti-flicker lighting (avoid 50–60 Hz flicker where possible), and gradual light adaptation. Occupational health letter offered.
- Sleep hygiene and stress management advice provided; signposted to relaxation apps. Consider sleep study if snoring worsens.
- Pre-conception: continue Folic Acid 5 mg daily; referral to the pre-conception neurology–obstetric clinic. We discussed relative medication risks and the importance of seizure control before pregnancy. 
- Bloods: U&E, LFT, FBC, Vitamin D level, and trough lamotrigine in 4 weeks after dose change.
- I will arrange an ambulatory EEG with extended photic protocol, with safeguards due to photosensitivity (short bursts, eye protection available). Patient consented.
- Safety advice revisited: bathing/showers, heights, operating machinery, and supervision when unwell. Provided SUDEP information sensitively.

Follow-up: Nurse specialist phone review in 4–6 weeks to assess the dose increase and light-triggered events; clinic review in 4 months, earlier if problems. Patient provided with 24/7 contact details for urgent concerns.

Thank you for your ongoing care. Please let me know if there are any concerns or if work-place adjustments are required. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: “nearly every night before sleep and now and then at first light,”

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes ongoing seizures with clustering around bedtime occurring on most evenings, plus additional events occasionally in early mornings and with bright light exposure. There is no explicit numeric count. The phrase "nearly every night before sleep" indicates frequent, near-daily events, but not precisely quantifiable. Given the vagueness and absence of exact numbers, the safest normalization is "multiple per week" rather than a precise daily rate or a cluster-based format, as the number per cluster is not specified.
seizure_frequency_number: “nearly every night before sleep and now and then at first light,”

flaws.description: Gold (multiple per week) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
